Client 2: Yeah, and like… what it means month to month. I don’t want it to turn into a spreadsheet marathon.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Jennifer, can you start with income at the household level—what do you consider your gross annual income all-in?
Client 1: Household gross is 84000 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—Jennifer, what’s your piece of that, roughly?
Client 1: Mine is $55,050 gross annually from my job.

Client 1: And I get a bonus quarterly—annualized it’s $62,800.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Let’s switch to spending. About what are your total monthly expenses, all-in—mortgage or rent, bills, groceries, everything?
Client 1: Total monthly expenses are 5650.
Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m noting that. Now, assets: if we look at investable assets—so cash, brokerage, retirement, that kind of thing—what’s the total you’re tracking?
Client 1: Investable assets total 295550.

Client 1: We have a small private markets position—$6,100—also on the advisor platform, in my name.
Advisor: Thanks. James, retirement accounts on your side—401(k) or IRA?
Client 2: Yeah, I’ve got a 401(k)/IRA bucket—$68,250—held on a retirement platform.
Advisor: And home—do you own your primary residence together?
Client 1: Yes, it’s joint, and the home value is $273,550. It’s listed through insurance, weirdly, but that’s where the value is coming from.

Client 1: Total monthly expenses are 5650.
Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m noting that. Now, assets: if we look at investable assets—so cash, brokerage, retirement, that kind of thing—what’s the total you’re tracking?
Client 1: Investable assets total 295550.
Advisor: Great. Let’s break that down just enough so I understand what’s where. Jennifer, do you have a taxable investment account?
Client 1: Yes—my taxable account is $186,600, and it’s on an advisor platform.

Client 2: Yeah, I’ve got a 401(k)/IRA bucket—$68,250—held on a retirement platform.
Advisor: And home—do you own your primary residence together?
Client 1: Yes, it’s joint, and the home value is $273,550. It’s listed through insurance, weirdly, but that’s where the value is coming from.
Advisor: That’s fine—we just need the value. Now liabilities: any loans or credit balances we should capture?
Client 2: There’s one loan. Balance is $2,400.

Client 2: Monthly payment is $250.

Client 2: No—number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Got it. Now taxes: what bracket are we planning around at the household level—do you know your tax bracket band?
Client 1: We’ve been using 35%.
Advisor: Okay. And just so you know where I’m coming from—my practice is an RIA, a registered investment advisor. Is that the advisory channel you’re looking for, or were you expecting more of a brokerage setup?
Client 2: RIA (registered investment advisor) is what we want, yeah.

Advisor: Got it. Now taxes: what bracket are we planning around at the household level—do you know your tax bracket band?
Client 1: We’ve been using 35%.
Advisor: Okay. And just so you know where I’m coming from—my practice is an RIA, a registered investment advisor. Is that the advisory channel you’re looking for, or were you expecting more of a brokerage setup?
Client 2: RIA (registered investment advisor) is what we want, yeah.
Advisor: Great. One sec—just noting that. While I’ve got you: earlier you mentioned no protection policies at the moment—still none for either of you, Jennifer and James?

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi James—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. Honestly, I want a clean picture—what we have, what we owe, and whether retirement timing is realistic.
Client 2: Yeah, and like… what it means month to month. I don’t want it to turn into a spreadsheet marathon.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Jennifer, can you start with income at the household level—what do you consider your gross annual income all-in?
Client 1: Household gross is 84000 a year.

Advisor: Perfect. Next one’s a little boring but important for forms—what state are you residents of right now?
Client 1: It’s listed as Other for state of residence.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And then risk—when markets swing around, how would you describe your overall investing comfort level? Conservative, moderate, growth… that kind of thing.
Client 2: I’d say growth, but like… not reckless.
Advisor: Yep, that makes sense—growth with some guardrails. Jennifer, does that match how you think about it too?

Advisor: And cash—any bank balances you consider part of the investable bucket?
Client 1: Yes, I’ve got $34,550 in a bank account, also on the advisor platform.
Advisor: Any other investments that are a little less traditional—private investments, that sort of thing?
Client 1: We have a small private markets position—$6,100—also on the advisor platform, in my name.
Advisor: Thanks. James, retirement accounts on your side—401(k) or IRA?

Advisor: Okay—thanks. Now I want to capture a couple household basics, Jennifer and James, so I’m not guessing. First one: is it just the two of you in the household—two adults?
Client 1: Yes, two adults—2.
Advisor: Great. And relationship status for planning purposes—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one’s a little boring but important for forms—what state are you residents of right now?

Client 1: Mine is $55,050 gross annually from my job.
Advisor: Thanks. James, and yours?
Client 2: Mine is $28,950 gross annually.
Advisor: Okay. And I know sometimes there’s salary versus bonus timing—do either of you have income that comes in monthly or quarterly?
Client 2: I have a salary that hits monthly, and the annual amount is $21,200.

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi James—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. Honestly, I want a clean picture—what we have, what we owe, and whether retirement timing is realistic.
Client 2: Yeah, and like… what it means month to month. I don’t want it to turn into a spreadsheet marathon.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Jennifer, can you start with income at the household level—what do you consider your gross annual income all-in?
Client 1: Household gross is 84000 a year.

Advisor: Great. While I’ve got you both, I just want to make sure we didn’t miss anything big: we’ve got the home, the one loan, and no protection policies to list right now—still all true?
Client 2: Yep, still true.
Advisor: Alright. Let’s shift back to income for a minute—James, can we pin down your work situation first? Are you currently employed, self-employed, retired—what’s the status?
Client 2: I’m employed.
Advisor: Got it. And in the household, I’ve got you listed as Jennifer’s spouse/partner—does that sound right?

Advisor: Alright. Let’s shift back to income for a minute—James, can we pin down your work situation first? Are you currently employed, self-employed, retired—what’s the status?

Client 2: I’m a business owner.
